Reference: Workflow.branch() - Mastra\ncreated: undefined, last updated: 2026-03-10, [Read more](https://mastra.ai/reference/workflows/workflow-methods/branch)\n\nThe `.branch()` method creates conditional branches between workflow steps, allowing for different paths to be taken based on the result of a previous step.\n## Usage example Direct link to Usage example\n```\nworkflow.branch([\n[async ({ context }) => true, step1],\n[async ({ context }) => false, step2],\n])\n```\n## Parameters Direct link to Parameters\n### steps :\n[() => boolean, Step]\nAn array of tuples, each containing a condition function and a step to execute if the condition is true\n## Returns Direct link to Returns"},{"type":"text","text":"# Control flow | Workflows | Mastra Docs\ncreated: undefined, last updated: 2026-04-08, [Read more](https://mastra.ai/docs/workflows/control-flow)\n\nUse `.branch()` to choose which step to run based on a condition.\nAll steps in a branch need the same `inputSchema` and `outputSchema` because branching requires consistent schemas so workflows can follow different paths.\nsrc/mastra/workflows/test-workflow.ts\n```\nconst step1 = createStep({...})\nconst stepA = createStep({\ninputSchema: z.object({\nvalue: z.number()\n}),\noutputSchema: z.object({\nresult: z.string()\n})\n});\nconst stepB = createStep({\ninputSchema: z.object({\nvalue: z.number()\n}),\noutputSchema: z.object({\nresult: z.string()\n})\n});\nexport const testWorkflow = createWorkflow({\ninputSchema: z.object({\nvalue: z.number()\n}),\noutputSchema: z.object({\nresult: z.string()\n})\n})\n.then(step1)\n.branch([\n[async ({ inputData: { value } }) => value > 10, stepA],\n[async ({ inputData: { value } }) => value <= 10, stepB]\n])\n.commit();\n```\nâ€¦\n...\nsrc/mastra/workflows/test-workflow.ts"},{"type":"text","text":"# Creating Conditional Workflows with Branching | CodeSignal Learn\ncreated: undefined, last updated: 2026-02-13, [Read more](https://codesignal.com/learn/courses/designing-mastra-workflows/lessons/creating-conditional-workflows-with-branching)\n\nThe branch() Method: Creating Conditional Paths\nTo create branches in a Mastra workflow, you use the `branch()` method.\nThis method takes an array of condition-step pairs, where each pair consists of:\n1. **A condition function** that returns `true` or `false`\n2. **A step** to execute if the condition is `true`\nHere's the basic syntax:\nFor example, with simple comparison conditions:\nThe workflow evaluates each condition in order and executes the first step whose condition returns `true`.\nExample: Branching in the Email Workflow\nLet's break down how branching works in our email processing workflow.\nStep 1: Classify the Intent\nFirst, we classify the email's intent:\nâ€¦\nKey takeaways:\n- Use `branch()` to create conditional paths in your workflows\n- Each branch consists of a condition function and a step to execute\n- Mastra automatically evaluates conditions and executes the appropriate step\n- Branching makes workflows more intelligent and adaptable to different scenarios"},{"type":"text","text":"# [FEATURE] Workflow branching V2 and first-class citizen support for nested workflows Â· Issue #2890 Â· mastra-ai/mastra\ncreated: 2025-03-11, last updated: 2025-08-24, [Read more](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/issues/2890)\n\n**Logical branching**\nWhile benefiting less, would still look visually easier to follow and could look something like:\n```\n// logical branching\nworkflow\n.then(step1)\n.if(\nasync ({ context }) => {\nconst step1Result = context.getStepResult(step1)\nreturn step1Result?.status === 'success'\n},\nworkflow({ name: 'if-wf' }).then(step2).then(step3),\nworkflow({ name: 'else-wf' }).then(step4).then(step5)\n)\n.then(step6)\n.then(step7)\n.commit()\n```\nMaking the `.if()` function optionally accept the truthy and falsy steps/sub-workflows to execute, calling `.then()` afterwards will automatically combine the conditional branches back into sequential execution independent of which branch was executed.\nTo see which branch yielded results, workflow name could be used to access step results.\nIn addition to easier branching, conditional branching via nested workflows also allows for a visually and cognitively easier to follow structure."},{"type":"text","text":"# Agentic Workflows with Mastra Workshop\ncreated: 2025-05-29, last updated: 2026-01-23, [Read more](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GE0V75Ixy1Q)\n\n{ts:1096} running  this  plan  activity  step.\nSo  a  question  from  the  chat  is  it\n{ts:1101} \n possible  to  run  several  several  steps \n{ts:1103} inside  one  branch  condition?\nYes  it  is  and  we're  going  to  see  that  in\n{ts:1107} \n the  next  example.\nâ€¦\n{ts:1310} Um  on  phase  value  it  looks  very  similar \n to  the  previous  example  because  we  have\n{ts:1314} \n this  branch  structure  all  the  same  steps \n{ts:1318} except  we've  replaced  plan  indoor \n activities  here  with  plan  both  workflow.\n{ts:1323} \n We  also  have  the  same  mapping  um  because"},{"type":"text","text":"# mastra/docs/src/content/en/examples/workflows/branching-paths.mdx at main Â· mastra-ai/mastra\ncreated: 2024-08-06, last updated: 2026-04-16, [Read more](https://github.com/mastra-ai/mastra/blob/main/docs/src/content/en/examples/workflows/branching-paths.mdx)\n\n|Example: Branching Paths | Workflows | Mastra Docs|Example of using Mastra to create workflows with branching paths based on intermediate results.|\n...\nThis example shows how to create a workflow that splits into separate paths, where each path executes different steps based on the output of a previous step.\nHere's the control flow diagram:\nâ€¦\n## Branching Paths and Chaining Steps\nNow let's configure the workflow with branching paths and merge them using the compound `.after([])` syntax.\n```\n// Create two parallel branches\nmyWorkflow\n// First branch\n.step(stepOne)\n.then(stepTwo)\n// Second branch\n.after(stepOne)\n.step(stepThree)\n.then(stepFour)\n// Merge both branches using compound after syntax\n.after([stepTwo, stepFour])\n.step(finalStep)\n.commit();\nconst { start } = myWorkflow.createRun();\nconst result = await start({ triggerData: { inputValue: 3 } });\nconsole.log(result.steps.finalStep.output.summary);\n// Output: \"The number 3 when doubled is not divisible by 5, and when doubled and incremented is divisible by 3.\"\n```"},{"type":"text","text":"# Orchestrating AI with Mastra.ai: A Deep Dive into Workflows - Shift Asia\ncreated: 2025-06-10, last updated: 2026-04-14, [Read more](https://shiftasia.com/community/orchestrating-ai-with-mastra-ai-a-deep-dive-into-workflows/)\n\n**Composing Workflows**\nSteps are orchestrated into workflows using methods like .then(), .parallel(), and .branch().\nThis composition defines the control flow and execution order.\n**Example:**\nâ€¦\n```\nworkflow.then(stepA).then(stepB);\n```\nâ€¦\n```\nworkflow.parallel([stepA, stepB]);\n```\n**3.\nConditional Branching: .branch()**\nExecutes steps based on evaluated conditions.\n```\nworkflow.branch([\n[({ inputData }) => inputData.value > 10, highValueStep],\n[({ inputData }) => inputData.value <= 10, lowValueStep],\n]);\n```\nâ€¦\n**5.\n...\n```\nâ€¦\n```\nconst stepA = createStep({ ... });\nconst stepB = createStep({ ... });\nconst workflow = createWorkflow({ ... })\n.then(stepA)\n.then(stepB)\n.commit();\n```\nâ€¦\n```\nconst mastra = new Mastra({\nworkflows: { myWorkflow: workflow },\n});\n```\nâ€¦\n```\nconst run = mastra.getWorkflow(\"myWorkflow\").createRun();\nconst result = await run.start({ inputData: { url: \"https://api.example.com\" } });\n```"},{"type":"text","text":"# A deep dive into Mastra AI workflows with code examples\ncreated: 2025-05-04, last updated: 2026-04-17, [Read more](https://khaledgarbaya.net/blog/mastering-mastra-ai-workflows/)\n\n```\nimport { mastra } from \"./path-to-your-workflow\";\nasync function runWorkflow() {\n// Get the workflow\nconst workflow = mastra.getWorkflow(\"myWorkflow\");\n// Create a run\nconst run = await workflow.createRunAsync();\n// Start the workflow with input data\nconst result = await run.start({\ninputData: {\ninputValue: 5,\n},\n});\nconsole.log(\"Result:\", result);\n// Should output something like:\n// Result: {\n// status: \"success\",\n// steps: {\n// doubleStep: { status: \"success\", result: { doubledValue: 10 } },\n// incrementStep: { status: \"success\", result: { finalValue: 11 } }\n// },\n// result: { finalValue: 11 }\n// }\n}\nrunWorkflow();\n```\nâ€¦\n```\ncurl --location 'http://localhost:4111/api/workflows/myWorkflow/run' \\\n--header 'Content-Type: application/json' \\\n--data '{ \"inputValue\": 5 }'\n```\nâ€¦\n### 2.\nConditional Branching\nOften, you need to determine the next step based on the outcome of a previous step:\nâ€¦\nThis workflow analyzes the sentiment of user input and then branches to different response steps based on the analysis."},{"type":"text","text":"# Master Human-in-the-Loop with Mastra - How to suspend and accept user input\ncreated: 2025-09-08, last updated: 2026-03-27, [Read more](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=aORuNG8Tq_k)\n\nIf we\n{ts:619} run the execution from the top and we ask where is order 99, for example, this time the categorize query step\n{ts:627} determines it's an order inquiry and therefore it branches this way to ask user for answer.\nThis step suspends the\n{ts:635} workflow and prompts the user, me in this case, to provide an answer manually freehand.\n...\n{ts:687} and so some of the code should look quite familiar.\nThe unique part is really this categorization and branching\n{ts:694} process.\nSo when we kick off the workflow with the customer support query, we first categorize the query by\n{ts:701} calling this categorize query step."},{"type":"text","text":"# Workflows - Mastra\ncreated: 2026-02-28, last updated: 2026-04-12, [Read more](https://mintlify.wiki/mastra-ai/mastra/concepts/workflows)\n\n```\nconst workflow = createWorkflow({\nid: 'user-approval',\ninputSchema: z.object({ userId: z.string() })\n})\n.then(checkUserStep) // Returns { approved: boolean }\n.branch({\n// Condition function\nwhen: ({ checkUserStep }) => checkUserStep.approved,\n// Steps to run if true\nthen: [approveStep, notifyStep],\n// Steps to run if false (optional)\notherwise: [rejectStep]\n})\n.commit();\n```\n### Multiple Branches\n```\nconst workflow = createWorkflow({\nid: 'payment-router',\ninputSchema: z.object({ amount: z.number(), method: z.string() })\n})\n.then(validatePaymentStep)\n.branch({\nwhen: ({ validatePaymentStep }) =>\nvalidatePaymentStep.method === 'card',\nthen: [processCardStep]\n})\n.branch({\nwhen: ({ validatePaymentStep }) =>\nvalidatePaymentStep.method === 'bank',\nthen: [processBankStep]\n})\n.commit();\n```\nâ€¦\n```\nconst workflow = createWorkflow(...)\n.then(createStep(fetchStep), { retries: 3 })\n.branch({\nwhen: ({ fetchStep }) => fetchStep.status === 'failed',\nthen: [logErrorStep, notifyStep]\n})\n.commit();\n```"}]}}]},{"role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"reasoning","text":"**Exploring Branch Syntax Options**\n\nI'm currently looking at the
